questions.title: Linear Beta Schedule
questions.description: Create a linear noise schedule that linearly interpolates beta values from beta_start to beta_end over T timesteps.
questions.info: The beta schedule controls how much noise is added at each timestep in the forward diffusion process. A linear schedule simply spaces beta values evenly between a small starting value (e.g., 1e-4) and a larger ending value (e.g., 0.02). This is the original schedule used in the DDPM paper by Ho et al. (2020). The choice of schedule significantly impacts generation quality, as it determines how quickly the data distribution is destroyed into pure Gaussian noise.

blanks.hint: Use torch.linspace to create T evenly spaced values from beta_start to beta_end.

questions.title: Compute Alpha from Beta
questions.description: Compute alpha_t = 1 - beta_t, the amount of signal retained at each step.
questions.info: In the forward diffusion process, alpha_t represents the fraction of the original signal preserved after one noising step. Since beta_t is the variance of the noise added, alpha_t = 1 - beta_t captures the complementary quantity: how much of the previous image is kept. Together, alpha and beta must sum to 1 at each timestep. This simple relationship is the foundation for all subsequent diffusion math, including the cumulative products used for direct sampling.

blanks.hint: alpha_t = 1 - beta_t for each timestep.

questions.title: Cumulative Product for Alpha Bar
questions.description: Compute alpha_bar_t = product of alpha_1 * alpha_2 * ... * alpha_t using a cumulative product.
questions.info: Alpha_bar_t (also written as \bar{alpha}_t) is the cumulative product of all alphas from timestep 1 to t. It represents the total fraction of the original signal remaining at timestep t. This quantity is essential because it allows us to sample x_t directly from x_0 without iterating through all intermediate steps, via x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon. As t increases, alpha_bar_t decreases toward 0, meaning the signal is progressively destroyed.

blanks.hint: Use torch.cumprod along dimension 0 to compute the running product.

questions.title: Forward Process: One Step q(x_t | x_{t-1})
questions.description: Implement one step of the forward diffusion process q(x_t | x_{t-1}) = N(x_t; sqrt(1 - beta_t) * x_{t-1}, beta_t * I).
questions.info: The forward process q(x_t | x_{t-1}) defines a Gaussian transition kernel that gradually adds noise to data. At each step, the mean is scaled by sqrt(1 - beta_t) (slightly shrinking the signal) and Gaussian noise with variance beta_t is added. This is a Markov chain where each step only depends on the previous one. The reparameterization trick lets us write this as x_t = sqrt(1 - beta_t) * x_{t-1} + sqrt(beta_t) * epsilon, where epsilon ~ N(0, I). This formulation makes the operation differentiable.

blanks.hint: The mean coefficient is sqrt(1 - beta_t).
The standard deviation is sqrt(beta_t).

questions.title: Reparameterization: Sample x_t Directly from x_0
questions.description: Sample x_t directly from x_0 using the reparameterization trick: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon.
questions.info: A key insight of DDPM is that we do not need to run the forward chain step by step. Because each step adds Gaussian noise and Gaussians compose nicely, we can jump directly from x_0 to any x_t in closed form. The formula x_t = sqrt(\bar{alpha}_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - \bar{alpha}_t) * epsilon elegantly decomposes x_t into a scaled original signal plus scaled noise. This makes training efficient since we can randomly sample any timestep t and immediately compute the noisy version.

blanks.hint: The signal coefficient is sqrt(alpha_bar_t).
The noise coefficient is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: DDPM Simplified Training Loss
questions.description: Implement the simplified DDPM training loss: L = E[||epsilon - epsilon_theta(x_t, t)||^2], the MSE between true noise and predicted noise.
questions.info: The DDPM paper showed that a simplified objective works best in practice: train the model epsilon_theta to predict the noise epsilon that was added to create x_t from x_0. The loss is simply the mean squared error between the actual noise sampled from N(0,I) and the model's prediction. This is mathematically equivalent (up to a weighting factor) to the variational lower bound on the data log-likelihood. The simplicity of this loss -- just predict the noise -- is one reason diffusion models became so practical and popular.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def diffusion_loss(model, x0, t, alpha_bar):
    """Compute simplified DDPM loss."""
    noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
    alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    x_t = torch.sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x0 + torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * noise
    predicted_noise = model(x_t, t)
    return {{b1}}

blanks.hint: Use F.mse_loss to compute MSE between the true noise and predicted noise.

questions.title: Cosine Noise Schedule
questions.description: Implement the cosine schedule for alpha_bar as proposed by Nichol & Dhariwal: alpha_bar_t = f(t)/f(0) where f(t) = cos((t/T + s)/(1+s) * pi/2)^2.
questions.info: The cosine schedule was introduced to fix a problem with the linear schedule: it destroys information too quickly in early timesteps and too slowly near the end. The cosine function provides a smoother, more gradual transition. The offset s (typically 0.008) prevents alpha_bar from being too small near t=T, which helps numerical stability. This schedule leads to better image quality because the model gets to see useful signal across a wider range of timesteps during training, rather than spending most of its capacity on nearly pure noise.
questions.code:
import torch
import math

def cosine_alpha_bar_schedule(T, s=0.008):
    """Compute alpha_bar using cosine schedule."""
    steps = torch.arange(T + 1, dtype=torch.float32)
    f = {{b1}}
    alpha_bar = f / f[0]
    return alpha_bar[1:]

blanks.hint: f(t) = cos((t/T + s) / (1 + s) * pi/2)^2. Use steps for t.

questions.title: Derive Betas from Alpha Bar
questions.description: Given a precomputed alpha_bar schedule, derive the per-step beta values. Since alpha_bar_t = prod(1 - beta_i), we have beta_t = 1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_{t-1}.
questions.info: When using a cosine or other non-linear schedule, we typically define alpha_bar directly and then derive the individual betas. The relationship alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar_{t-1} * (1 - beta_t) can be rearranged to beta_t = 1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_{t-1}. In practice, we also clip beta to a maximum value (e.g., 0.999) to prevent numerical issues when alpha_bar values are very small near the end of the diffusion process. This derived beta can then be used in the reverse sampling process.

blanks.hint: beta_t = 1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_{t-1}.

questions.title: Signal-to-Noise Ratio
questions.description: Compute the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at timestep t. SNR_t = alpha_bar_t / (1 - alpha_bar_t).
questions.info: The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at timestep t quantifies the relative strength of the original signal versus the added noise in x_t. Since x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon, the signal power is alpha_bar_t and noise power is (1-alpha_bar_t), giving SNR = alpha_bar_t / (1-alpha_bar_t). The SNR monotonically decreases from a large value (nearly pure signal) at t=0 to near zero (nearly pure noise) at t=T. The log-SNR is often used for analysis and for defining improved loss weightings.

blanks.hint: SNR = alpha_bar_t / (1 - alpha_bar_t).
Take the natural logarithm of SNR.

questions.title: Posterior Variance of Reverse Process
questions.description: Compute the posterior variance beta_tilde_t = beta_t * (1 - alpha_bar_{t-1}) / (1 - alpha_bar_t), used in the reverse diffusion step.
questions.info: The posterior q(x_{t-1} | x_t, x_0) is a Gaussian whose variance beta_tilde_t can be computed in closed form. This variance is fixed (not learned in basic DDPM) and depends on beta_t, alpha_bar_t, and alpha_bar_{t-1}. The formula beta_tilde_t = beta_t * (1 - alpha_bar_{t-1}) / (1 - alpha_bar_t) arises from Bayes' rule applied to the Gaussian forward transitions. This quantity is used during the reverse sampling process to add the right amount of stochasticity when denoising from x_t to x_{t-1}.

blanks.hint: beta_tilde_t = beta_t * (1 - alpha_bar_{t-1}) / (1 - alpha_bar_t). Use alpha_bar_prev for alpha_bar_{t-1}.

questions.title: Variance of x_t Given x_0
questions.description: Compute the variance of x_t given x_0. Since x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*eps, the variance of the noise component is (1 - alpha_bar_t).
questions.info: When we sample x_t from x_0 using the reparameterization trick, the noise term is sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon where epsilon~N(0,I). The variance of this noise component is therefore (1-alpha_bar_t)*I. As t increases, alpha_bar_t decreases, so the variance grows from nearly 0 (at t=0, almost no noise) to nearly 1 (at t=T, almost pure unit-variance Gaussian noise). This monotonic increase in variance is what drives the forward process to gradually transform data into noise.

blanks.hint: The variance of x_t given x_0 is (1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Generate Noisy Images at Multiple Timesteps
questions.description: Given an image x0, generate noisy versions at several timesteps using the closed-form forward process.
questions.info: Visualizing the forward process at different timesteps helps build intuition about how diffusion models work. At early timesteps, the image is barely corrupted and fine details start to blur. At middle timesteps, the overall structure is still visible but heavily noised. Near the final timestep T, the image is almost indistinguishable from pure Gaussian noise. This progressive corruption is exactly what the reverse process (the neural network) must learn to undo, step by step.
questions.code:
import torch

def generate_noisy_images(x0, alpha_bar, timesteps):
    """Generate noisy versions of x0 at given timesteps.
    Returns a list of noisy images."""
    noisy_images = []
    for t in timesteps:
        ab_t = alpha_bar[t]
        noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
        x_t = {{b1}} * x0 + {{b2}} * noise
        noisy_images.append(x_t)
    return noisy_images

blanks.hint: Signal coefficient is sqrt(alpha_bar_t).
Noise coefficient is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Gaussian Noise Sampling (NumPy)
questions.description: Sample Gaussian noise with the same shape as an input image using NumPy.
questions.info: Gaussian (normal) noise is the fundamental building block of diffusion models. The forward process adds standard normal noise (mean=0, variance=1) at each step. In NumPy, np.random.randn returns samples from the standard normal distribution. The noise must match the shape of the input data so it can be added element-wise. Understanding how to sample and manipulate Gaussian noise is a prerequisite for implementing any diffusion model.

blanks.hint: Use np.random.randn with the shape of x unpacked, or np.random.standard_normal.

questions.title: Full Schedule Computation Pipeline
questions.description: Compute all diffusion schedule quantities (betas, alphas, alpha_bar, sqrt_alpha_bar, sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar) from a linear schedule.
questions.info: In practice, all schedule-related quantities are precomputed once and stored for use during training and sampling. This avoids redundant computation at each training step. The key derived quantities are: alphas (1-beta), alpha_bar (cumulative product of alphas), sqrt(alpha_bar) for scaling x_0, and sqrt(1-alpha_bar) for scaling noise. These precomputed tensors are typically registered as buffers in the PyTorch model so they move to the correct device automatically.

blanks.hint: alpha_t = 1 - beta_t.
alpha_bar is the cumulative product of alphas along dim 0.

questions.title: DDPM Training Step
questions.description: Implement a single training step for DDPM: sample random timesteps, add noise, predict noise, compute loss.
questions.info: Each DDPM training iteration involves: (1) sampling a batch of random timesteps uniformly from [0, T), (2) sampling Gaussian noise, (3) creating noisy inputs x_t using the reparameterization trick, (4) having the model predict the noise from x_t and t, and (5) computing MSE loss between true and predicted noise. The uniform sampling of timesteps ensures the model learns to denoise at all noise levels equally. This simple recipe -- combined with the architectural inductive biases of U-Net -- produces remarkably high-quality generative models.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def train_step(model, optimizer, x0, sqrt_alpha_bar, sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar, T):
    batch_size = x0.shape[0]
    t = {{b1}}
    noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
    
    s_ab = sqrt_alpha_bar[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    s_omab = sqrt_one_minus_alpha_bar[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    x_t = s_ab * x0 + s_omab * noise
    
    pred_noise = model(x_t, t)
    loss = {{b2}}
    
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()

blanks.hint: Sample random timesteps uniformly from 0 to T-1 for each item in the batch.
MSE loss between actual noise and predicted noise.

questions.title: Posterior Mean for Reverse Step
questions.description: Compute the posterior mean mu_tilde(x_t, x_0) = (sqrt(alpha_bar_{t-1}) * beta_t / (1 - alpha_bar_t)) * x_0 + (sqrt(alpha_t) * (1 - alpha_bar_{t-1}) / (1 - alpha_bar_t)) * x_t.
questions.info: The posterior mean is a weighted combination of x_0 and x_t. It comes from applying Bayes' rule to the forward Gaussian transitions q(x_t|x_{t-1}) and q(x_{t-1}|x_0). The two coefficients trade off between the original data estimate and the current noisy observation. In practice, since we do not know x_0, we first estimate it from the predicted noise: x_0_hat = (x_t - sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon_theta) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t), then plug that into this formula. This is the core of the DDPM reverse sampling procedure.

blanks.hint: Coefficient for x0: sqrt(alpha_bar_{t-1}) * beta_t / (1 - alpha_bar_t).
Coefficient for x_t: sqrt(alpha_t) * (1 - alpha_bar_{t-1}) / (1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Predict x_0 from Predicted Noise
questions.description: Given x_t and predicted noise epsilon_theta, recover the predicted x_0: x_0 = (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon_theta) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t).
questions.info: Since x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon, we can algebraically solve for x_0 once we have an estimate of epsilon. The model predicts epsilon_theta, so x_0_hat = (x_t - sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon_theta) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t). This predicted x_0 is used in the reverse sampling process to compute the posterior mean. In practice, the predicted x_0 is often clipped to [-1, 1] to improve stability, since the true data lies in that range for normalized images.

blanks.hint: x_0 = (x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * eps_theta) / sqrt(alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Forward Process in NumPy
questions.description: Implement the forward diffusion reparameterization in NumPy: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * epsilon.
questions.info: Implementing the forward process in NumPy helps solidify the mathematical foundation without the abstraction of PyTorch tensors. The formula x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon is the same regardless of framework. Using NumPy is useful for prototyping, visualization, and understanding the math. The key numpy function np.sqrt computes element-wise square roots, and np.random.randn samples from the standard normal distribution.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def forward_diffusion_numpy(x0, alpha_bar_t):
    """Add noise to x0 at level determined by alpha_bar_t."""
    noise = np.random.randn(*x0.shape)
    x_t = {{b1}} * x0 + {{b2}} * noise
    return x_t, noise

blanks.hint: Signal scaling: sqrt(alpha_bar_t).
Noise scaling: sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Sinusoidal Timestep Embedding
questions.description: Create sinusoidal positional embeddings for diffusion timesteps, similar to Transformer positional encodings.
questions.info: Diffusion models need to condition on the timestep t so the network knows the current noise level. Sinusoidal embeddings, borrowed from the Transformer architecture, map integer timesteps to continuous vector representations. Even dimensions use sin and odd dimensions use cos, with frequencies that decrease geometrically. This gives the model a smooth, continuous representation of time that generalizes across timesteps. The embedding dimension is typically matched to the model's hidden dimension (e.g., 128 or 256).
questions.code:
import torch
import math

def sinusoidal_embedding(t, dim):
    """Create sinusoidal timestep embeddings.
    t: (batch_size,) integer timesteps
    dim: embedding dimension (must be even)
    """
    half_dim = dim // 2
    emb_scale = {{b1}} / (half_dim - 1)
    emb = torch.exp(torch.arange(half_dim, dtype=torch.float32) * -emb_scale)
    emb = t[:, None].float() * emb[None, :]
    return {{b2}}

blanks.hint: Use log(10000) as the base frequency, same as in Transformers.
Concatenate sin and cos of the scaled timesteps along the last dimension.

questions.title: Compare Linear and Cosine Schedules
questions.description: Compute and compare alpha_bar values for both linear and cosine schedules to observe their different noise progression profiles.
questions.info: Comparing schedules reveals their fundamentally different noise progression profiles. The linear schedule's alpha_bar drops quickly at first (destroying fine details early) and flattens near the end. The cosine schedule provides a much more uniform decrease, preserving signal longer at early timesteps. This can be quantified by looking at the SNR at various points: the cosine schedule maintains higher SNR at early timesteps and decreases more smoothly. In practice, the cosine schedule produces better FID scores because the model can effectively learn at more timesteps.
questions.code:
import torch
import math

def compare_schedules(T=1000):
    # Linear schedule
    betas_lin = torch.linspace(1e-4, 0.02, T)
    alphas_lin = 1.0 - betas_lin
    alpha_bar_lin = {{b1}}
    
    # Cosine schedule
    steps = torch.arange(T + 1, dtype=torch.float32)
    f = torch.cos((steps / T + 0.008) / 1.008 * math.pi / 2) ** 2
    alpha_bar_cos = {{b2}}
    
    return alpha_bar_lin, alpha_bar_cos[:T]

blanks.hint: Cumulative product of alphas gives alpha_bar.
Normalize f by f[0] and skip the first element to get T values.

questions.title: Quadratic Beta Schedule
questions.description: Create a quadratic noise schedule where beta values follow a quadratic curve from beta_start to beta_end over T timesteps.
questions.info: A quadratic beta schedule spaces the beta values along a squared curve rather than a straight line. This means noise increases slowly at first and then accelerates toward the end. The implementation takes the square of linearly spaced values between sqrt(beta_start) and sqrt(beta_end). This schedule was explored as an alternative to the linear schedule and can preserve more signal in early timesteps while still reaching the same final noise level.

blanks.hint: Linearly space values between sqrt(beta_start) and sqrt(beta_end), then square the result.

questions.title: Sigmoid Beta Schedule
questions.description: Implement a sigmoid-based beta schedule that transitions smoothly from beta_start to beta_end using a sigmoid function.
questions.info: The sigmoid beta schedule uses the logistic sigmoid function to create an S-shaped curve for the beta values. It maps linearly spaced values through a sigmoid, then scales and shifts to fit between beta_start and beta_end. The sigmoid's flat tails mean that beta changes slowly at the start and end, with faster changes in the middle. This can be advantageous because it provides a smoother transition than the linear schedule while being simpler to implement than the cosine schedule.

blanks.hint: Apply the sigmoid function to map values into the (0, 1) range.

questions.title: SNR in Decibels from Alpha Bar
questions.description: Compute the signal-to-noise ratio in decibels (dB) from the alpha_bar schedule. SNR_dB = 10 * log10(alpha_bar / (1 - alpha_bar)).
questions.info: Expressing the SNR in decibels provides a more interpretable scale for human reasoning about noise levels. The conversion uses 10*log10 of the linear SNR. A positive dB value means the signal dominates, zero dB means equal signal and noise power, and negative dB means noise dominates. Diffusion processes typically span a wide range, from high positive dB (clean data) to large negative dB (nearly pure noise). This representation is borrowed from signal processing and is useful for comparing different schedules.

blanks.hint: Convert linear SNR to decibels using 10 * log10(snr).

questions.title: Marginal Distribution Mean and Std at Time t
questions.description: Compute the mean and standard deviation of the marginal distribution q(x_t | x_0) = N(sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0, (1 - alpha_bar_t) * I).
questions.info: The marginal distribution of x_t given x_0 is a Gaussian whose mean is sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 and whose standard deviation is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t). The mean shrinks the original data by a factor of sqrt(alpha_bar_t), which decreases over time. The standard deviation grows as sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) increases. At t=0, the mean is approximately x_0 and std is approximately 0 (no noise). At t=T, the mean is approximately 0 and std is approximately 1 (pure noise).

blanks.hint: The mean of q(x_t|x_0) is sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0.
The std of q(x_t|x_0) is sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Transition Kernel Parameters
questions.description: Extract the mean coefficient and variance for the forward transition kernel q(x_t | x_{t-1}) = N(sqrt(alpha_t) * x_{t-1}, beta_t * I) from the schedule.
questions.info: The forward transition kernel q(x_t | x_{t-1}) is fully characterized by two parameters: a mean coefficient sqrt(alpha_t) = sqrt(1 - beta_t) that scales the previous state, and a variance beta_t that determines the noise magnitude. These parameters are derived directly from the beta schedule. Understanding these kernel parameters is important because they define each individual step of the Markov chain. The mean coefficient being less than 1 ensures the process gradually shrinks the signal, while the added noise ensures it converges to a standard Gaussian.

blanks.hint: The mean coefficient of q(x_t|x_{t-1}) is sqrt(alpha_t).
The variance of the transition kernel is simply beta_t.

questions.title: Verify Markov Chain Composition
questions.description: Verify that running the forward process step-by-step for k steps produces the same distribution as the closed-form marginal q(x_k | x_0) by comparing the mean and variance.
questions.info: A defining property of the diffusion forward process is that it is a Markov chain where Gaussians compose in closed form. Running k individual steps q(x_1|x_0), q(x_2|x_1), ..., q(x_k|x_{k-1}) yields the same distribution as the direct marginal q(x_k|x_0). This can be verified by showing that the effective signal scaling is the product of individual scalings (i.e., sqrt(alpha_bar_k)) and the effective noise variance is (1 - alpha_bar_k). This composability is what makes training DDPM efficient since we can jump to any timestep directly.

blanks.hint: The composed signal scaling is the product of sqrt(alpha_i) for each step.

questions.title: KL Divergence Between Two Gaussians (Forward Steps)
questions.description: Compute the KL divergence between two diagonal Gaussian distributions KL(N(mu1, sigma1^2 I) || N(mu2, sigma2^2 I)) in closed form, as used to compare forward process distributions.
questions.info: The KL divergence between two Gaussians with diagonal covariance has a well-known closed-form expression. For d-dimensional Gaussians, KL(q||p) = 0.5 * sum(sigma1^2/sigma2^2 + (mu2-mu1)^2/sigma2^2 - 1 + log(sigma2^2/sigma1^2)). This formula is essential in the ELBO derivation for diffusion models, where each term in the variational bound involves a KL between Gaussian forward and reverse transitions. Computing this analytically avoids the need for Monte Carlo estimation of these KL terms.
questions.code:
import torch

def kl_divergence_gaussians(mu1, sigma1, mu2, sigma2):
    """KL(N(mu1, sigma1^2 I) || N(mu2, sigma2^2 I)).
    All inputs are tensors of the same shape."""
    var1 = sigma1 ** 2
    var2 = sigma2 ** 2
    return {{b1}}

acceptable: 0.5 * torch.sum(var1 / var2 + (mu2 - mu1) ** 2 / var2 - 1 + torch.log(var2 / var1))
blanks.hint: KL = 0.5 * sum(var1/var2 + (mu2-mu1)^2/var2 - 1 + log(var2/var1)).

questions.title: ELBO Reconstruction Term
questions.description: Compute the reconstruction term of the ELBO: -E_q[log p(x_0 | x_1)], modeled as a discretized Gaussian log-likelihood at the final denoising step.
questions.info: The ELBO for diffusion models decomposes into three parts: a reconstruction term, KL terms for intermediate steps, and a prior matching term. The reconstruction term measures how well the model can recover x_0 from x_1 (the least noisy latent). For images, this is typically modeled as a discretized Gaussian: the model predicts a mean mu_theta(x_1, 1) and the log-likelihood is computed by integrating the Gaussian density over pixel bins of width 1/255. In the simplified DDPM loss, this term is approximated by MSE, but exact evaluation uses the discretized formulation.
questions.code:
import torch

def reconstruction_log_likelihood(x0, predicted_mean, log_scale):
    """Discretized Gaussian log-likelihood for 8-bit images.
    x0: ground truth in [-1, 1], predicted_mean: model output."""
    scale = torch.exp(log_scale)
    bin_width = 1.0 / 127.5  # width of a pixel bin in [-1,1]
    centered = x0 - predicted_mean
    upper = (centered + bin_width / 2) / scale
    lower = (centered - bin_width / 2) / scale
    # CDF difference gives probability in each bin
    log_probs = torch.log({{b1}} + 1e-12)
    return log_probs.sum(dim=[1, 2, 3]).mean()

blanks.hint: The Gaussian CDF can be approximated via the sigmoid of the standardized bounds. CDF difference = sigmoid(upper) - sigmoid(lower).

questions.title: ELBO Prior Matching Term
questions.description: Compute the prior matching term of the ELBO: KL(q(x_T | x_0) || p(x_T)), where p(x_T) = N(0, I) is the standard Gaussian prior.
questions.info: The prior term in the diffusion ELBO measures how close the final noised distribution q(x_T|x_0) is to the standard Gaussian prior N(0,I). Since q(x_T|x_0) = N(sqrt(alpha_bar_T)*x_0, (1-alpha_bar_T)*I), this KL can be computed in closed form. For a well-designed schedule with alpha_bar_T close to 0, this term is very small because the forward process has nearly destroyed all signal. This term has no learnable parameters and acts as a constant in training, but it is needed for exact log-likelihood evaluation.
questions.code:
import torch

def prior_kl(x0, alpha_bar_T):
    """KL(q(x_T | x_0) || N(0, I))."""
    mean = torch.sqrt(alpha_bar_T) * x0
    var = 1.0 - alpha_bar_T
    # KL(N(mean, var*I) || N(0, I))
    return {{b1}}

blanks.hint: KL(N(mu, sigma^2 I) || N(0, I)) = 0.5 * sum(mu^2 + sigma^2 - 1 - log(sigma^2)).

questions.title: Variational Lower Bound: Single KL Term
questions.description: Compute a single KL term L_{t-1} = KL(q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0) || p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)) of the variational lower bound, where both distributions are Gaussian with known variances.
questions.info: Each intermediate term L_{t-1} in the variational lower bound compares the true posterior q(x_{t-1}|x_t,x_0) with the learned reverse transition p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t). Both are Gaussians with the same fixed variance (beta_tilde_t), so the KL reduces to a scaled squared difference between their means. Specifically, L_{t-1} = (1/(2*beta_tilde_t)) * ||mu_tilde - mu_theta||^2. This is why the simplified DDPM loss (noise prediction MSE) works: it is proportional to this KL term up to a timestep-dependent weighting factor.

blanks.hint: KL between two Gaussians with equal variance simplifies to (1/(2*var)) * ||mu1 - mu2||^2 summed over dimensions.

questions.title: Bits Per Dimension from NLL
questions.description: Convert a negative log-likelihood (in nats, base e) to bits per dimension, the standard metric for evaluating likelihood-based generative models on images.
questions.info: Bits per dimension (bpd) is the standard metric for comparing likelihood-based generative models. It measures the average number of bits needed to encode each dimension (pixel channel) of the data under the model. To convert from nats (natural log units) to bits, we divide by ln(2). Then we divide by the total number of dimensions (C*H*W for images). Lower bpd means the model assigns higher likelihood to the data. For reference, DDPM achieves around 3.7 bpd on CIFAR-10, while autoregressive models can reach below 3.0 bpd.

blanks.hint: Divide NLL in nats by ln(2) to convert to bits, then by num_dims for per-dimension.

questions.title: Noise Level Grid (NumPy)
questions.description: Given an image as a NumPy array, create a grid of noisy versions at evenly spaced alpha_bar values from nearly clean to nearly pure noise.
questions.info: Visualizing how an image degrades at different noise levels helps build intuition for what the diffusion model must learn to reverse. By choosing evenly spaced alpha_bar values (e.g., 0.95, 0.75, 0.50, 0.25, 0.05), we can see the full progression from minimal to extreme corruption. At alpha_bar=0.95, only fine details are lost. At alpha_bar=0.5, the image is heavily corrupted but recognizable. At alpha_bar=0.05, it is nearly indistinguishable from pure noise. This visualization is a standard diagnostic tool.

blanks.hint: Apply the forward diffusion formula: sqrt(ab)*image + sqrt(1-ab)*noise.

questions.title: Clamp Predicted x_0
questions.description: After predicting x_0 from the noise estimate, clamp the result to the valid data range [-1, 1] to improve sampling stability.
questions.info: When the model predicts noise and we algebraically solve for x_0, the predicted values can sometimes fall outside the valid data range of [-1, 1] for normalized images. Clamping (clipping) the predicted x_0 to this range is a simple but effective trick that improves visual quality and prevents artifacts in generated samples. This technique is sometimes called 'dynamic thresholding' in its more sophisticated forms, as used in Imagen. The basic version simply uses torch.clamp to enforce hard bounds.

blanks.hint: Use torch.clamp to restrict x0_pred to the range [-1, 1].

questions.title: Prepare Noise Prediction Network Input
questions.description: Prepare the input for the noise prediction network by creating x_t from x_0 and returning x_t along with the sampled timesteps and true noise for loss computation.
questions.info: During training, each forward pass requires assembling several quantities: a batch of random timesteps, the corresponding alpha_bar values reshaped for broadcasting, random Gaussian noise, and the noisy images x_t. The timesteps are sampled uniformly, the noise is standard Gaussian, and x_t is constructed via the reparameterization trick. All these quantities must be on the correct device and have compatible shapes. This input preparation function encapsulates the boilerplate code that precedes every model forward pass.
questions.code:
import torch

def prepare_training_input(x0, alpha_bar, T):
    """Prepare (x_t, t, noise) for the noise prediction network."""
    B = x0.shape[0]
    t = torch.randint(0, T, (B,), device=x0.device)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
    ab_t = {{b1}}
    x_t = torch.sqrt(ab_t) * x0 + torch.sqrt(1.0 - ab_t) * noise
    return x_t, t, noise

blanks.hint: Index alpha_bar with t and reshape to (-1, 1, 1, 1) for broadcasting with image tensors.

questions.title: Channel Concatenation Conditioning
questions.description: Implement channel concatenation conditioning where a conditioning signal (e.g., a segmentation mask or low-res image) is concatenated along the channel dimension with x_t before feeding to the U-Net.
questions.info: Channel concatenation is one of the simplest conditioning mechanisms for diffusion models. The conditioning signal is resized to match the spatial dimensions of x_t and then concatenated along the channel axis. For example, if x_t has shape (B, 3, H, W) and the condition has shape (B, C_cond, H, W), the U-Net input becomes (B, 3+C_cond, H, W). This approach is used in image-to-image translation tasks like super-resolution (concatenating the low-res input) and inpainting (concatenating the masked image and binary mask). The U-Net's first convolution must accept the increased channel count.

blanks.hint: Concatenate along dim=1 (the channel dimension) for image tensors in (B, C, H, W) format.

questions.title: Cross-Attention Conditioning Layer
questions.description: Implement a cross-attention layer that conditions intermediate U-Net features on an external embedding (e.g., text embeddings from CLIP), as used in Stable Diffusion.
questions.info: Cross-attention is the primary mechanism for text-to-image conditioning in models like Stable Diffusion. The U-Net features serve as queries, while the conditioning embeddings (e.g., from a text encoder) serve as keys and values. The attention output is a weighted combination of value vectors, where the weights depend on how each spatial location in the image relates to each token in the text. This mechanism allows the model to spatially distribute semantic information from the text prompt across the generated image.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class CrossAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, context_dim, heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.heads = heads
        self.head_dim = dim // heads
        self.to_q = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.to_k = nn.Linear(context_dim, dim)
        self.to_v = nn.Linear(context_dim, dim)
        self.to_out = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
    
    def forward(self, x, context):
        """x: (B, N, dim), context: (B, S, context_dim)"""
        B, N, _ = x.shape
        q = self.to_q(x).view(B, N, self.heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.to_k(context).view(B, -1, self.heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.to_v(context).view(B, -1, self.heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        attn = {{b1}}
        attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
        out = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, N, -1)
        return self.to_out(out)

blanks.hint: Scaled dot-product attention: (Q @ K^T) / sqrt(d_k). Transpose the last two dims of k.

questions.title: FID Score: Feature Extraction Setup
questions.description: Set up the Inception-v3 feature extractor used for computing the Frechet Inception Distance (FID) by extracting features from the penultimate pooling layer.
questions.info: The FID score compares statistics of generated images to real images using features from a pre-trained Inception-v3 network. Features are extracted from the 2048-dimensional average pooling layer (before the final classification head). The model is set to eval mode with gradients disabled for efficiency. Both real and generated images must be resized to 299x299 pixels and normalized to the ImageNet range. FID measures the Frechet distance between two multivariate Gaussians fitted to these feature distributions.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torchvision.models import inception_v3

def setup_fid_extractor():
    """Create Inception-v3 feature extractor for FID."""
    model = inception_v3(pretrained=True)
    model.fc = {{b1}}
    model.eval()
    return model

blanks.hint: Replace the final fully-connected layer with nn.Identity() to get the 2048-d features instead of class logits.

questions.title: FID Score: Compute Feature Statistics
questions.description: Compute the mean and covariance of Inception features for a set of images, which are the statistics needed to compute the FID score.
questions.info: The FID score models the distribution of Inception features as a multivariate Gaussian, characterized by its mean vector and covariance matrix. Given a set of N feature vectors of dimension D (typically D=2048), we compute the empirical mean (a D-dimensional vector) and the empirical covariance matrix (a DxD matrix). These statistics are computed separately for both the real and generated image sets. The FID is then the Frechet distance between the two fitted Gaussians. Larger datasets yield more reliable statistics.

blanks.hint: Compute the mean across samples (axis=0) to get a D-dimensional vector.
Compute the covariance with rowvar=False since each row is a sample.

questions.title: V-Parameterization (Velocity Prediction)
questions.description: Implement the v-parameterization where the model predicts v = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * epsilon - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * x_0, and recover both x_0 and epsilon from the predicted v.
questions.info: The v-parameterization, introduced in the Progressive Distillation paper by Salimans & Ho, defines the prediction target as v_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*epsilon - sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*x_0. This is a rotation of the (x_0, epsilon) pair in signal-noise space. The advantage is that predicting v gives a more balanced learning signal across all noise levels compared to predicting epsilon (which is hard at low noise) or x_0 (which is hard at high noise). Given the predicted v, we can recover both x_0 and epsilon analytically using the inverse rotation.

blanks.hint: x_0 = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_t - sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * v.
epsilon = sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t) * x_t + sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * v.

questions.title: SNR-Weighted Loss (Min-SNR-gamma)
questions.description: Implement the min-SNR-gamma loss weighting strategy that clips the SNR-based weight to a maximum of gamma, providing more balanced training across timesteps.
questions.info: The simplified DDPM loss implicitly uses a loss weighting of 1 for all timesteps, but this is suboptimal because the difficulty of predicting noise varies greatly across noise levels. The min-SNR-gamma strategy (Hang et al., 2023) uses weight = min(SNR_t, gamma) / SNR_t, where gamma is typically 5. This clips the weight for high-SNR (low-noise) timesteps, which would otherwise dominate training. The result is faster convergence and better sample quality because the model allocates more capacity to the harder, high-noise timesteps.

blanks.hint: Clamp (or take minimum of) SNR to gamma, then divide by SNR to get the weight.

questions.title: Posterior Log Variance (Clipped)
questions.description: Compute the log of the posterior variance with numerical clipping for stability, as done in the original DDPM implementation.
questions.info: Taking the log of the posterior variance is necessary for the reverse sampling step and for KL divergence computation. However, the posterior variance at t=1 involves alpha_bar_prev=1 and 1-alpha_bar_prev=0, making the log undefined. The DDPM implementation clips the posterior variance to a minimum value (typically beta_1, the first beta) before taking the log. This is a practical detail that prevents NaN values during sampling. The log variance is used rather than the variance itself for numerical stability when the values are very small.

blanks.hint: Clamp the posterior variance to a small minimum value to avoid taking log(0).

questions.title: Reverse Process: Sample x_{t-1} from x_t
questions.description: Implement one step of the reverse diffusion process: given x_t and the model's noise prediction, sample x_{t-1}.
questions.info: The reverse sampling step is the core of image generation with DDPM. Given x_t and the model's noise prediction, we first estimate x_0, then compute the posterior mean mu_tilde. Finally, we add noise scaled by the posterior standard deviation (except at t=0 where no noise is added). The formula is x_{t-1} = mu_tilde + sqrt(beta_tilde_t) * z, where z ~ N(0,I). This stochastic reverse step is run sequentially from t=T down to t=1, progressively denoising the sample.
questions.code:
import torch

def reverse_step(model, x_t, t, betas, alphas, alpha_bar, alpha_bar_prev, posterior_var):
    """One step of DDPM reverse sampling."""
    pred_noise = model(x_t, t)
    # Predict x_0
    ab_t = alpha_bar[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    x0_pred = (x_t - torch.sqrt(1.0 - ab_t) * pred_noise) / torch.sqrt(ab_t)
    x0_pred = torch.clamp(x0_pred, -1, 1)
    # Posterior mean
    b_t = betas[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    ab_prev = alpha_bar_prev[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    a_t = alphas[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    mu = torch.sqrt(ab_prev) * b_t / (1.0 - ab_t) * x0_pred + torch.sqrt(a_t) * (1.0 - ab_prev) / (1.0 - ab_t) * x_t
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_t)
    pv = posterior_var[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    # No noise at t=0
    nonzero_mask = {{b1}}
    return mu + nonzero_mask * torch.sqrt(pv) * noise

blanks.hint: Create a mask that is 1 when t > 0 and 0 when t = 0, reshaped for broadcasting.

questions.title: Cumulative Product Alpha Bar (NumPy)
questions.description: Compute alpha_bar as the cumulative product of alphas using NumPy.
questions.info: NumPy provides np.cumprod for computing cumulative products, analogous to torch.cumprod in PyTorch. Given an array of alpha values, the cumulative product at index t gives the total signal retention alpha_bar_t = alpha_1 * alpha_2 * ... * alpha_t. This is useful when prototyping diffusion schedules in NumPy before moving to PyTorch. The cumulative product operation is performed along a specified axis, typically axis 0 for a 1D array of schedule values.

blanks.hint: Use np.cumprod to compute the running product of alphas.

questions.title: Linear Beta Schedule with Warmup
questions.description: Implement a linear beta schedule that starts with a warmup phase of constant low beta for a specified number of steps before linearly increasing.
questions.info: A warmup phase at the start of the beta schedule keeps beta at a small constant value for the first few timesteps before transitioning to the linear ramp. This ensures that the earliest reverse steps (which produce the finest details) operate at very low noise levels, giving the model more precision for high-frequency features. The warmup concept is borrowed from learning rate scheduling in optimization. After the warmup period, the schedule linearly increases from beta_start to beta_end over the remaining timesteps.

blanks.hint: The ramp covers the remaining T - warmup_steps timesteps.

questions.title: x_0 Prediction Loss (Alternative Parameterization)
questions.description: Implement a training loss where the model directly predicts x_0 instead of the noise epsilon, computing MSE between true x_0 and predicted x_0.
questions.info: While DDPM uses noise prediction (epsilon-parameterization), an alternative is to train the model to directly predict x_0 from x_t. The loss is L = E[||x_0 - x_0_theta(x_t, t)||^2]. This x_0-prediction parameterization can be advantageous at high noise levels where predicting x_0 directly is more stable. Some models like DALL-E 2 use this parameterization. The two parameterizations are mathematically related through the reparameterization trick, but they can have different training dynamics due to implicit loss weighting differences.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def x0_prediction_loss(model, x0, t, alpha_bar):
    """Training loss for x_0-prediction parameterization."""
    noise = torch.randn_like(x0)
    alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t].reshape(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    x_t = torch.sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x0 + torch.sqrt(1.0 - alpha_bar_t) * noise
    x0_pred = model(x_t, t)
    return {{b1}}

blanks.hint: MSE loss between the true x_0 and the model's predicted x_0.

questions.title: Convert Between Noise and x_0 Parameterizations
questions.description: Given a model that predicts noise, convert its output to an x_0 prediction, and vice versa. This allows switching between parameterizations at inference time.
questions.info: Noise prediction and x_0 prediction are two sides of the same coin. Given the forward process x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon, predicting one uniquely determines the other. To convert noise prediction to x_0: x_0 = (x_t - sqrt(1-ab)*eps_pred)/sqrt(ab). To convert x_0 prediction to noise: eps = (x_t - sqrt(ab)*x0_pred)/sqrt(1-ab). This interchangeability means you can train with one parameterization and sample with another, which is useful for methods like DDIM that work with x_0 predictions.

blanks.hint: eps = (x_t - sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x0_pred) / sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t).

questions.title: Learned Variance Model Output Split
questions.description: Split the model output into noise prediction and learned variance components, as used in Improved DDPM (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021).
questions.info: The original DDPM uses a fixed posterior variance, but Improved DDPM learns the variance by having the model output both the noise prediction and a variance interpolation factor. The model output has twice the number of channels: the first half predicts noise, the second half predicts a log-variance interpolation parameter v. The actual log-variance is then interpolated between log(beta_tilde) and log(beta) using v. This learned variance significantly improves log-likelihood and enables fewer sampling steps without quality degradation.

blanks.hint: Split the tensor along the channel dimension (dim=1), each half having C channels.

questions.title: Interpolate Between Fixed Variances
questions.description: Interpolate between the two fixed variance bounds (beta_tilde and beta) in log-space using the learned variance parameter, as in Improved DDPM.
questions.info: Improved DDPM parameterizes the learned variance as an interpolation between two known bounds in log-space: log(beta_tilde_t) (the posterior variance, which is the lower bound) and log(beta_t) (the upper bound). The model outputs a parameter v in [0, 1] (after applying sigmoid), and the log-variance is v*log(beta) + (1-v)*log(beta_tilde). This constrained parameterization ensures the learned variance stays in a reasonable range. Training the variance requires the full VLB loss, not just the simplified MSE, making it slightly more complex to implement.

blanks.hint: Linear interpolation: v * log(beta) + (1-v) * log(beta_tilde).

questions.title: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) Model Update
questions.description: Update an EMA (shadow) copy of the model parameters, which is standard practice in diffusion model training for better sample quality.
questions.info: Exponential Moving Average of model parameters is a crucial trick for diffusion models. Instead of using the final trained weights, an EMA copy maintains a running weighted average: theta_ema = decay * theta_ema + (1 - decay) * theta. The decay is typically 0.9999 or 0.999. The EMA model produces significantly better samples than the raw trained model because it smooths out the optimization trajectory. This is a standard practice in nearly all modern diffusion model implementations, from DDPM to Stable Diffusion.

blanks.hint: EMA update: theta_ema = decay * theta_ema + (1 - decay) * theta.

questions.title: FID Score: Frechet Distance Computation
questions.description: Compute the Frechet Inception Distance between two sets of feature statistics (mean and covariance) using the closed-form Frechet distance formula.
questions.info: The Frechet distance (also called Wasserstein-2 distance) between two multivariate Gaussians N(mu1, C1) and N(mu2, C2) is d^2 = ||mu1-mu2||^2 + Tr(C1 + C2 - 2*(C1*C2)^{1/2}). The square root of the product of covariance matrices is computed via eigendecomposition or scipy's sqrtm function. Lower FID indicates that generated images are more similar to real images in terms of both mean feature activations and their correlations. FID is the most widely used metric for evaluating unconditional image generation quality, though it has known limitations with small sample sizes.

blanks.hint: FID = ||mu1-mu2||^2 + Tr(C1 + C2 - 2*sqrt(C1*C2)). The covmean variable holds sqrt(C1*C2).
